258
10Technology in Counseling
LEARNING OBJECTIVES
10.1 	Demonstrate an understanding of why it is
important for counselors to understand how their
clients are using technology and social media.
10.2 	List advantages and disadvantages of counselors
providing virtual counseling instead of in-person
counseling.
10.3 	Describe advances in counselors’ use of technology
and discuss potential concerns.
10.4 	Explain the ethical issues involved in counselors
using electronic means to communicate with their
clients.
FOCUS QUESTIONS
1. What do you think about
counselors providing counseling
services to clients using Zoom or a
similar type of compressed video
technology?
2. What problems do clients bring to
counseling sessions that are directly
related to their use of social media
or other forms of technology?
3. What steps do counselors need
to take to ensure that counseling
records kept electronically are
secure and that client privacy is
maintained?
4. What do you think about the idea
of an avatar or a robot that mimics
human interactions counseling a
client?
Counselors’ use of a variety of technologies has increased
significantly during the current decade. The use of com-
pressed video technology to provide counseling services is
too new for the popular or professional literature to have set-
tled on terminology that will be used regarding the phenom-
enon. Counseling using compressed video is being referred
to as virtual counseling, telemental health counseling, tele-
therapy, online counseling, remote counseling, e-counseling,
and many other terms. In this chapter we use the term virtual
counseling to refer to the phenomenon of counselors pro-
viding services to clients where both can see and hear each
other while they are located in different places.
The Impact of the COVID-19
Pandemic on Technology in
Counseling
Since the last edition of this textbook, the world of counseling
has changed dramatically due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Using compressed video and electronic communications
rather than in-person sessions for counseling, supervision,
and the teaching of counseling to graduate students had
been considered experimental and a questionable practice.

-- 281 of 593 --

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 	259
Then suddenly, almost overnight, when the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown occurred, that practice
became the norm. The pivot from traditional to digital approaches in the counseling profession was
surprisingly successful, but many concerns were expressed by counselors, supervisors, and counselor
educators about whether the transition from in-person to online counseling was ethical, legal, or pro-
fessional (Becnel et al., 2023; Lipschitz et al., 2022).
Professionals continue to question whether counseling delivered virtually is as effective as
in-person counseling (McCord et al., 2022; Uhl et al., 2022). However, research studies are being
published that suggest virtual counseling is as effective as in-person counseling with specific popu-
lations and client concerns. Despite uncertainties about the effectiveness of virtual counseling, the
practice is taking place everywhere. Even though in-person counseling services have resumed to
some degree, it appears that virtual counseling is here to stay in the United States. Artificial intelli-
gence has been introduced into the counseling profession, and in some instances, avatars that mimic
humans instead of human counselors are interacting with clients.
In this chapter, we summarize the ethical and legal issues surrounding the use of technology
in counseling and pay particular attention to guidelines that have been developed by professional
associations, certification boards, state licensure boards, and governmental agencies regarding the
use of technology in the delivery of mental health services. The COVID-19 pandemic has had an
impact on clients’ use of technology and virtual counseling and on counselors’ use of technology.
We discuss each of these areas in this chapter.
THE IMPACT OF TECHNOLOGY ON
THE COUNSELING PROFESSION
Even before COVID-19, technological advances in society, especially the internet, were having a sig-
nificant impact on the profession of counseling (Hertlein et al., 2014; Kraus et al., 2010; Ziv, 2014).
The counseling profession has established a substantial presence on the internet: Technology offers
counselors abundant opportunities to access virtual libraries for researching the latest information on
client problems and effective counseling techniques, as well as to collaborate and consult with other
professionals around the world. The National Board for Certified Counselors (NBCC), the Council for
Accreditation of Counseling and Related Educational Programs (CACREP), and many of the divisions
of the American Counseling Association (ACA) have homepages on the internet, which means that you
can communicate quickly and directly with professional groups that are working to strengthen counsel-
ing as a profession. Videoconferencing on the internet enables you and counselors everywhere to obtain
further preparation without having to travel great distances. You can also subscribe to mailing lists that
allow you to share experiences, ask questions, and exchange information and ideas.
Technology is being used today in mental health in ways that were previously unimagined. In a
study completed prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, Steele et al. (2020) found that school counselors
have increased their use of technology for student planning and are continuing to use technology in
responsive services. Counselors are providing real-time, two-way virtual counseling sessions to clients
who are on the other side of the world; clients are presenting issues related specifically to their social
media activities such as being addicted to the internet, being the victim of cyberbullying, and leaving
current relationships for ones they found online. In addition, counselors are utilizing biofeedback in
their practices (Crockett et al., 2017), and clients with dementia are being tracked with GPS devices
(Landau & Werner, 2012). Virtual reality interventions and smartphone apps are being used to treat
clients with anxiety disorders (Firth et al., 2018) and other conditions. Smartphones allow counselors
to utilize multiple forms of technology in their practices (Epstein & Bequette, 2013). Legal and ethical
challenges abound as counselors utilize technology in creative ways in their practices each day.

-- 282 of 593 --

260 	Section 2 • Issues
The various forms of technology that have been developed and are still being created all have
the same basic purposes—to facilitate the transfer of information and enhance the ability to commu-
nicate with others. In this transfer and communication process, situations inevitably occur in which
sensitive or confidential information might be inappropriately accessed. Every form of technology
discussed in this section creates records or recordings of information that, if not treated carefully,
could lead to the privacy of clients being compromised.
Certainly, counselors want to maximize their effectiveness by utilizing as many forms of
technology as possible (Sampson & Bloom, 2001). It is also imperative that counselors educate
themselves regarding the subtle ways in which their clients’ privacy might be affected by the use
of technology (Cottone et al., 2022). When the ACA Code of Ethics (2014) was last revised, an
entirely new section was added to address technology issues (i.e., Section H: Distance Counseling,
Technology, and Social Media). Many other standards related to technology issues are found
throughout the 2014 Code.
The introduction to Section H of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), which is devoted
to ethical issues related to technology, states that counselors, whether or not they want to,
must adapt: “Counselors actively attempt to understand the evolving nature of the profession
with regard to distance counseling, technology, and social media and how such resources may
be used to better serve their clients. Counselors strive to become knowledgeable about these
resources.” Standard H.1.a. continues, “Counselors who engage in the use of distance counsel-
ing, technology, and/or social media develop knowledge and skills regarding related technical,
ethical and legal considerations (e.g., special certifications, additional course work).” So, the
Code of Ethics requires that counselors actively attempt to understand the new issues related to
technology in counseling, and further, that if they engage in the use of technology, they develop
knowledge and skills for doing so.
In the following discussion, we identify a number of areas in which technology is having an
impact on the counseling process, and we provide guidelines for ensuring that the privacy of clients
is protected. In addition, counselors’ personal and professional use of technology is reviewed in
relation to ethical and legal obligations.
Client Use of Technology
Clients use technology in numerous ways of which counselors need to be aware. Clients go online
to use various types of social media and entertainment technology and to engage in self-monitoring
and self-improvement, all without counselor supervision.
SOCIAL MEDIA 	The 	Code 	of 	Ethics 	(ACA, 	2014) 	Glossary 	defines 	social 	media 	as
“ technology-based forms of communication of ideas, beliefs, personal histories, etc. (e.g., social
networking sites, blogs).” Social media have been categorized by a number of experts, without
consensus regarding what is included in social media (Boundless, 2014; Grahl, 2014; White, 2014).
Grahl (2014) has listed six types of social media: (1) social networks, (2) bookmarking sites,
(3) social news, (4) media sharing, (5) microblogging, and (6) blog comments and forums. These
categories are not discrete, and sometimes they overlap in content and function.
• Social networks allow you to connect with other people and usually include a user’s profile
and various ways to interact with other users. The most popular social networks are Facebook,
X (formerly Twitter), Instagram, WhatsApp, TikTok, and Snapchat.
• Bookmarking sites offer services that allow you to save, organize, and manage links to websites
and resources on the internet. The most popular bookmarking sites are X (formerly Twitter),
Pinterest, Pocket, Google Bookmarks, and Mix (previously StumbleUpon).

-- 283 of 593 --

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 	261
• Social news includes services people use to post various news items or links to articles. Users
then vote, and the items receiving the most votes are displayed the most prominently. The most
popular social news sites are Reddit, Digg, and Propeller.
• Media sharing allows users to upload and share media, including photos and videos. The most
popular media sharing sites are Facebook, YouTube, and WhatsApp.
• Microblogging is characterized by short updates that are sent to those who subscribe.
The most popular microblogging sites are X (formerly Twitter), Tumblr, Mastodon, and
LinkedIn.
• Forums allow members to post messages that are available to other members. Blog comments
are similar, except that they are attached to blogs and include discussions about the blog. There
are too many popular blogs and forums to identify the most popular ones.
Despite the lack of consensus about what is included in social media, everyone agrees that it is
very popular and that people of all ages are involved in social media in a variety of formats. Client use
of social media is a pressing concern for school counselors (Casares & Binkley, 2022; Giordano et al.,
2022; Mullen et al., 2014) and child and adolescent counselors. As social media began to be devel-
oped, some counselors were unsure as to how to respond to their clients’ posts. Counselors questioned
whether they had an obligation to view what their clients were posting or were not supposed to pry into
their clients’ social media posts. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) takes the position that “Counselors
respect the privacy of their clients’ presence on social media unless given consent to view such infor-
mation” (ACA, 2014, §H.6.c.). As a result of this standard, it generally is not appropriate for coun-
selors to view posts made by their clients. If clients give their permission for their counselors to view
their social media posts, however, counselors may look at them. Even if counselors have been granted
permission by their clients to view their social media posts, doing so could lead to liability problems. If
counselors view their clients’ social media posts and see that a minor is engaging in behaviors that are
concerning, the counselor might have a responsibility to notify their parents or guardians.
Many of the problems that clients now bring to counseling are directly related to the phenom-
enon of social media. A few of these problems include social media addiction, the fear of missing
out (FOMO), social isolation, cyberbullying, sexting, and identity theft.
Social media addiction or digital addiction occurs when the personal and professional lives of
individuals are negatively affected because they cannot stay away from social media sites (Didelot
et al., 2012; Griffiths, 2013). Social media use has been directly related to social isolation (Primack
et al., 2017), reduced productivity, unhealthy social relationships, and reduced life satisfaction (Sun
& Zhang, 2021). Hormes et al. (2014) concluded, in a study of undergraduate students, that the use
of social networking sites is potentially addictive. Carlisle et al. (2016) have suggested that internet
addiction is a process addiction, or a compulsive behavior that targets a particular activity (such as
gambling or binge eating). Chibbaro et al. (2019) indicated that counselors need to be able to iden-
tify young people who have developed digital addiction. They noted that the signs and symptoms of
individuals who have digital addiction include loss of control over time, cravings for engagement,
and social problems. Young (2023) has developed an instrument to help assess internet addiction
and has identified these symptoms: neglecting friends and family; losing sleep to stay online; feel-
ing ashamed, anxious, or depressed as a result of online behavior; backaches, headaches, or carpal
tunnel syndrome; and withdrawing from social or pleasurable activities. Researchers are continuing
to investigate how social media addiction develops so that prevention approaches can be developed
(Sun & Zhang, 2021).
Younger clients who have been using technology their entire lives are sometimes referred
to as digital natives (Gallo et al., 2016). Some adults may not have heard of FOMO, but
youngsters have (Blackwell et al., 2017; White, 2013). FOMO, the fear of missing out, occurs

-- 284 of 593 --

262 	Section 2 • Issues
when people are anxious because they believe everyone else is having more fun than they are
(Wortham, 2011). A result of FOMO is people wanting to stay continually connected to those
they know and monitoring what others are doing (Fioravanti et al., 2021; Gori et al., 2023).
Results of studies indicate that those in the age range from 18 to 30 have the highest rates of
FOMO. Researchers at universities in England (University of Essex, 2013) and the United States
have developed a test that allows individuals to determine the extent to which FOMO negatively
affects their ability to enjoy what they are doing rather than worry about what others are up to
(Przybylski et al., 2013).
Social networking sites such as Facebook have been identified as causing social isolation (Parigi
& Henson, 2014). However, Grieve et al. (2013), after completing two studies on the topic, concluded
that Facebook use could lead to developing and maintaining relationships and to positive psychologi-
cal outcomes. Further research is needed to determine the effects, both positive and negative, of cli-
ents’ use of Facebook.
CYBERBULLYING 	Cyberbullying has been identified as a problem that some clients bring to coun-
seling sessions (Burnham et al., 2011; Florang, 2020; Güllü et al., 2023; Kowalski et al., 2014;
Lanzillo, 2021; Olenik-Shemesh et al., 2012; McGuckin et al., 2013; Wright, 2012). Florang (2020)
has suggested that past mistakes and misconceptions have hindered efforts to prevent cyberbully-
ing. Perpetrators of cyberbullying have been found to be deterred more by the threat of punishment
from their parents and the school and less by the threat of punishment from the police (Patchin &
Hinduja, 2018). Helping youngsters understand that adults need to know when they are being bul-
lied is important in helping victims. According to Olenik-Shemesh et al. (2012), victims of cyber-
bullying often feel hopeless and are reluctant to report the abuse because they fear their parents
will take away their access to social media. Florang (2020) has advised administrators and parents
to avoid minimizing the impact cyberbullying has on victims, stop punishing victims by taking
away their privileges and freedom, and teach them the skills necessary to overcome cyberbully-
ing. After investigating the bullying policies on nine social media platforms, Tinstman et al. (2019)
concluded that the terms used vary and are unclear, the language used was punitive and negative,
cultural awareness was limited to anti-discrimination, and mental health
concerns were addressed but seldom associated with cyberbullying.
SEXTING 	An area of concern that has emerged for adolescent prob-
lems related to technology is the practice of sexting, which involves
sending sexual messages or images through text messaging (Ibtisam,
2017; Kosenko, 2017; McEachern et al., 2012), along with sextortion.
Sextortion has been defined by O’Malley and Holt (2022)
as a threat to distribute intimate, sexual materials unless a victim
agrees to some type of demand such as paying money. The prac-
tice of youth sexting and sextortion has been increasing over time
(Mori, 2022). After analyzing 39 studies of youth under the age of 18,
Madigan et al. (2018) reported that 14.8% had sent sexts and 27.4%
had received sexts. In addition, 12% of the young people studied had
forwarded sexts without the consent of the persons who had originally
posted them. Utilizing a national sample of adolescents between 12 and
17 years old, Giordano et al. (2022) found that approximately 53% had
seen pornography, almost 15% had sent a sext, and 24.3% had received

-- 285 of 593 --

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 	263
a sext. There is thus a need to provide youngsters with information about the legal consequences
of sexting and advice about what to do if they receive a sexting image (Mitchell et al., 2012; Wolfe
et al., 2014). Lunceford (2011) observed that despite the highly publicized suicides that have
occurred as a result of sexting, teenagers seem to be unaware of the potential for embarrassment and
humiliation when sexts are shared with unintended viewers or even posted on the internet.
VIRTUAL COUNSELING
The traditional process for counseling involves a counselor and one or more clients meeting face-to-face
in a professional office. When the internet came into existence, some counselors began offering vir-
tual counseling. After the COVID-19 pandemic arrived, almost all counseling services were delivered
virtually through compressed video, and today clients can find virtual counseling services easily if
they prefer that approach over meeting in person with counselors. In addition to virtual counseling,
counseling is being provided through telephone calls (Dadfar & Lester, 2021; Rowen et al., 2022);
synchronous online interaction, better known as ChatGPT (Dowling & Richwood, 2013); and text
messages and email exchanges (Fang et al., 2018; Nieuwboer et al., 2015).
A growing number of telehealth platforms, also known as practice management software,
afford counselors a range of features for delivering virtual counseling services to clients. Popular
platforms include Doxy.me, SimplePractice, thera-LINK, TheraPlatform, and TherapyNotes. When
selecting a platform, counselors should identify their essential technology needs and consider the
suitability of various platforms for serving the broadest range of clients. Some other important con-
siderations include monthly cost, ease of use for client and clinician, BAA- and HIPAA-compliance,
functionality for individual and multi-person sessions, dependability of technology and customer
service, and optional features such as electronic session notes, scheduling, billing and payment
processing, and secure file sharing.
10-1 The Case of Abby
Abby specializes in counseling individuals who have experienced the death of a twin brother
or sister after reaching adulthood. Abby has written many popular magazine articles about her
work and has appeared on national television talk shows. She lives in a small town. She has been
getting many requests from all over the United States to counsel clients who have lost an adult
twin brother or sister. Abby has been counseling clients locally using Zoom since the COVID-19
pandemic began, but she has never counseled anyone from out of state. She now wants to offer
virtual counseling services for a fee to clients who do not live in her state.
• What are some of the ethical and legal considerations Abby should address before begin-
ning to offer virtual counseling services to out-of-state clients?
Discussion: First, Abby should be licensed for independent private counseling practice in the
state in which she lives. In addition, she should investigate state laws and regulations in the
states in which potential clients whom she wishes to serve are located to determine if she is
eligible to offer residents counseling services without being licensed in that state. Most states
require that counselors be licensed to practice in that state to offer counseling services to
residents of the state or that counselors meet exceptions that have been created in licensure

-- 286 of 593 --

264 	Section 2 • Issues
The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) includes a number of standards related to providing virtual
counseling to clients. To begin, counselors must ensure that clients understand that they have a choice
and do not have to engage in virtual counseling if they prefer not to (ACA, 2014, §H.2.a.). This stan-
dard also requires that counselors allow clients to determine whether they are willing to use social
media or technology within the counseling process. During the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown
period, many clients were not able to choose between virtual counseling and in-person counseling
because in-person counseling was suspended. Today, clients still may have to accept virtual counseling
because that is the only option offered. This standard requiring that clients be given a choice between
in-person and virtual counseling most likely will be revised when the ACA Code of Ethics is updated.
Informed Consent
When virtual counseling, social media, or other types of technology are used in counseling, the informed
consent obtained from clients must go beyond the typical requirements and must include, according
to the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), the following elements: “distance counseling credentials, physi-
cal location of practice, and contact information; risks and benefits of engaging in the use of distance
counseling, technology, and/or social media; possibility of technology failure and alternate methods of
service delivery; anticipated response time; emergency procedures to follow when the counselor is not
available; time zone differences; cultural and/or language differences that may affect delivery of ser-
vices; possible denial of insurance benefits; and social media policy” (ACA, 2014, §H.2.a.).
The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) also contains a provision that assumes that electronic records
are less secure than paper records. Standard H.2.b. states, “Counselors acknowledge the limitations
of maintaining the confidentiality of electronic records and transmissions. They inform clients that
individuals might have authorized or unauthorized access to such records or transmissions (e.g., col-
leagues, supervisors, employees, information technologists).” It is possible for electronic records to
be accessed by unauthorized persons, but counselors who use standard precautions such as encrypted
communications and password-protected records storage will be able to keep their clients’ privacy
board rules. An example of one narrow exception is the rule in Ohio that allows counselors
licensed out of state to provide counseling services to Ohio residents for a short period of time
(https://cswmft.ohio.gov/). As more states pass statutes to participate in the interstate coun-
seling compacts, the problem of providing counseling to clients across state lines will lessen.
However, Abby probably will have to restrict her practice to clients living in her state unless she
is willing to become licensed as a counselor in other states as well.
It will be important for Abby to determine whether her present professional liability insur-
ance policy covers virtual counseling activities. If not, she should obtain a policy that does. Abby
should also review all of the virtual counseling standards in the 2014 ACA Code of Ethics to
ensure that she is practicing in an ethical manner. These standards are discussed in detail later
in this chapter. Finally, Abby should consider taking continuing education workshops or courses
that focus specifically on virtual counseling to increase her awareness of the issues and to gain
skills in that area. To increase her credibility in this new area of practice, she should consider
earning the Board Certified-TeleMental Health Provider (BC-TMH) credential from the Center for
Credentialing and Education (www.cce-global.org), an affiliate of the NBCC (www.nbcc.org). For
best practice, and prior to accepting virtual counseling clients, it would be wise for Abby to seek
clinical supervision from an experienced virtual counselor.

-- 287 of 593 --

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 	265
secure to the extent possible. Even when only in-person counseling took place and records were kept
on paper, client privacy could never be 100% guaranteed by counselors. Counselors are expected
only to take reasonable measures to protect client privacy.
Despite a number of concerns regarding virtual counseling that are discussed later in this
chapter, ever since the COVID-19 pandemic, the practice has become an accepted approach to
counseling in the United States. Training in virtual counseling is now required by some state licen-
sure boards such as Louisiana’s (https://www.lpcboard.org/), and virtual counseling training pro-
grams are readily available to counselors. NBCC (2018), through the Center for Credentialing and
Education, offers the Board Certified-TeleMental Health Provider (BC-TMH) credential for coun-
selors who wish to demonstrate their skills in this particular area of counseling (https://www.cce-
global.org/credentialing/bctmh).
Advantages of Virtual Counseling
Virtual counseling presents several advantages compared to face-to-face counseling in the same
physical space. These advantages include: (a) increased accessibility of services for those who would
otherwise find it difficult to receive services, such as low-income clients; elderly persons whose
mobility is limited; individuals who are chronically ill or severely physically disabled; rural or iso-
lated individuals; and clients who suffer from agoraphobia, social phobia, extreme introversion, or
serious physical illnesses; (b) perceived anonymity may decrease client anxiety, thus encouraging
people to ask for help and increasing the extent to which clients are willing to share intimate issues;
(c) greater freedom in scheduling; (d) a reduction in lost work time and travel time for clients; and
(e) facilitation of improved preparation by allowing better choice of written words over spoken ones
(Bouchard et al., 2000; Haberstroh et al., 2007, 2014; Hamburger & Ben-Artzi, 2000; Heinlen et al.,
2003; Kiriakaki et al., 2022; Lawson et al., 2022; Lee, 2010; Lin et al., 2022; National Alliance on
Mental Illness, 2014; Reeves, 2011; Rummell & Joyce, 2010; Schultze, 2006; Shaw & Shaw, 2006).
After reviewing the results of numerous surveys completed after the COVID-19 epidemic
began, Moreno et al. (2020) concluded that the general public is experiencing increased symptoms
of depression, anxiety, and stress. These symptoms are attributed to stressors including life disrup-
tion, fear of illness, and fear of negative economic effects. According to Egede et al. (2020), impor-
tant challenges in the United States since COVID-19 have been slow implementation of telemental
health services and state licensing laws that forbid practitioners from servicing clients across state
lines. These laws in most states were lifted during the pandemic, and there is a call to make changes
permanent that would give access to virtual counseling services to all citizens.
Several researchers have studied clients’ reactions and attitudes toward virtual counseling.
Some scholars have noted positive responses by clients to virtual counseling services (Cook &
Doyle, 2002; Day & Schneider, 2002; Frye et al., 2022). Manhal-Baugus (2001) reported that 90%
of clients surveyed believed that their online therapists had been helpful to them. Lee et al. (2023)
found that clients in a Midwest community counseling center rated virtual counseling favorably
when they were able to apply counseling sessions to real-life circumstances and had frequent coun-
seling sessions, and when they perceived that their counselor had skills that helped them in their sit-
uations. Scott et al. (2022) found that persons with depression reported no difference in satisfaction
between virtual counseling and face-to-face immediately post-treatment, or after 3 or 12 months,
and Lee et al. (2023) concluded that virtual counseling may be a viable alternative for persons
with depression. Reese et al. (2006) found that 58% of 186 survey respondents who had received
both virtual counseling and physically present, face-to-face counseling preferred virtual counsel-
ing. Further, 96% of their respondents said they would seek virtual counseling again, whereas only
63.1% said they would seek face-to-face counseling in the future. The results of this study suggest

-- 288 of 593 --

266 	Section 2 • Issues
that some clients prefer virtual counseling over face-to-face counseling. Other researchers have
found that although clients were satisfied with virtual counseling, they preferred face-to-face coun-
seling (Leibert et al., 2006; Rochlen et al., 2004).
In addition to client satisfaction research, outcome research is beginning to demonstrate the
clinical effectiveness of virtual counseling (e.g., Barak et al., 2008; Griffiths & Christensen, 2006;
Labardee, 2009; Richards & Vigano, 2013). Results of studies suggest that virtual counseling is as
effective as in-person counseling. Studies have also concluded that virtual counseling is effective
in counseling combat veterans (Brief et al., 2018); treating persons with depression (Königbauer
et al., 2017); counseling persons with binge-eating problems (Rom et al., 2022); treating individuals
with substance use disorders (Sistad et al., 2023); and group counseling using dialectical behavior
therapy (Bean et al., 2022). Gerton et al. (2023) found that clients who transitioned from in-person
counseling to virtual counseling fared well.
Limitations and Boundaries
In the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), §H.4. focuses on distance counseling relationships—now com-
monly called virtual counseling relationships—that are delivered through the use of technology
applications. These applications are listed as including, but not being limited to, “computer hard-
ware and/or software, telephones and applications, social media and internet-based applications
and other audio and/or video communication, or data storage devices or media.” This section of the
2014 Code gives counselors guidelines related to boundaries, the ability of clients to use technology,
the effectiveness of technology services rendered, access to services, and communication differ-
ences when using technology.
Standard H.4.b. of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) requires counselors to “discuss and estab-
lish professional boundaries with clients regarding the appropriate use and/or application of tech-
nology and the limitations of its use within the counseling relationship (e.g., lack of confidentiality,
times when not appropriate to use).” It would appear that the boundaries to which this standard is
referring might include setting limits on when clients may or may not communicate by email mes-
sages or texts, for example.
Counselors are required to make reasonable efforts to ensure that clients with whom they use
technology understand how to use it and have the capacity to use it (ACA, 2014, Code of Ethics,
§H.4.c.). This standard requires that counselors “follow up with clients to correct possible mis-
conceptions, discover appropriate use, and assess subsequent steps.” Counselors must also provide
information regarding gaining access to technology applications when they are being utilized in the
counseling relationships.
When counselors or clients determine that virtual counseling services are not effective,
§H.4.d. of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) requires that “counselors consider delivering services
face-to-face. If the counselor is not able to provide face-to-face services (e.g., lives in another state),
the counselor assists the client in identifying appropriate services.” This section of the ACA Code
of Ethics needs to be updated. During the lockdown period of the COVID-19 pandemic, no face-to-
face counseling services were available, and today face-to-face counseling services may be difficult
to locate or may not be available to some clients.
Standard H.4.f. (ACA, 2014) requires that counselors warn clients about possible misunder-
standings when virtual counseling is being utilized. This standard states, “Counselors consider the
differences between face-to-face and electronic communication (nonverbal and verbal cues) and
how these may affect the counseling process. Counselors educate clients on how to prevent and
address potential misunderstandings arising from the lack of visual cues and voice intonations when
communicating electronically.”

-- 289 of 593 --

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 	267
The virtual counseling provisions of the 2014 ACA Code of Ethics provide ethical guidelines
for practice. However, there are many issues in virtual counseling that are unresolved and are being
discussed in the literature. A legal question exists as to where virtual counseling takes place: in the
location of the counselor, the location of the client, or both locations. Which laws govern the counsel-
ing process related to issues such as reporting abuse is another question. Because virtual counseling
is still relatively new, these questions will not be resolved until lawsuits are filed for cases in which
virtual counseling was used. Counselors who provide virtual counseling services need to ensure that
their professional liability insurance provides coverage for virtual counseling (Reeves, 2011).
Counselors who consider offering counseling services over the internet should ensure that
they are not violating state laws (in the state of the counselor or of the client) that require counsel-
ors to be licensed. Standard H.1.b. (ACA, 2014) provides this guidance regarding following legal
requirements when using technology in counseling: “Counselors who engage in the use of distance
counseling, technology, and social media within their counseling practice understand that they may
be subject to laws and regulations of both the counselor’s practicing location and the client’s place
of residence. Counselors ensure that their clients are aware of pertinent legal rights and limitations
governing the practice of counseling across state lines or international boundaries.”
A Rapidly Changing Environment
A potential problem with virtual counseling is related to the fact that the field is changing so rapidly.
The pace of change will make it difficult for counselors to maintain a full measure of competence
in its use. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, Trepal et al. (2007) suggested that counselor educa-
tors prepare counselors to provide virtual counseling because it had become so accepted in society.
Since the pandemic, all counselor education programs are preparing counselors to provide virtual
counseling services because that type of training is essential. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014,
§H.1.a.) says, “Counselors who engage in the use of distance counseling, technology, and/or social
media develop knowledge and skills regarding related technical, ethical, and legal considerations
(e.g., special certifications, additional course work).”
Although guidelines for the use of technology in health and mental health are proliferating,
not all guidelines are helpful to counseling practitioners. Many guidelines related to the use of
technology in counseling overlap and are repetitive. For example, Anvari et al. (2023) reviewed
60 existing guidelines for the provision of virtual medical care. About half of the guidelines were
published after the COVID-19 pandemic began. Most of the guidelines (92%) specified the prac-
tice area in which their guidelines applied. Many of the guidelines addressed confidentiality issues,
technology requirements, and patient consent. The researchers concluded that the quality of the
published guidelines was poor. Anvari et al. concluded that best practices for telemedicine include
paying attention to confidentiality, patient consent, preparation before a visit, and clear patient
communication. The review of virtual medical care completed by Anvari et al. (2023) provides
some insight into similar guidelines that are being published regarding virtual counseling in that
guidelines for the use of virtual counseling that will be developed in the future also need to be as
client-focused as possible.
The ACA Code of Ethics (2014) includes a section on the use of technology in counseling
entitled, “Section H: Distance Counseling, Technology, and Social Media.” However, since this
code was adopted in 2014, the COVID-19 pandemic occurred and the use of technology in coun-
seling has exploded, which has necessitated the development of additional recommendations. A
number of professional associations, certification bodies, and other entities have developed guide-
lines for the use of technology in mental health counseling. Figure 10-1 lists some of the docu-
ments that are providing guidance to counselors in their use of technology.

Chapter 10 • Technology in Counseling 	269
With virtual counseling, there is no reliable method of regulating the services offered, of ensuring
the competence of the providers, or of protecting consumers from ill-advised internet-based services
(International Society for Mental Health Online, 2008; Peterson et al., 2007; Shaw & Shaw, 2006).
On the other hand, advances are being made in regulating virtual counseling services delivered across
state lines. The Telehealth Certification Institute (https://www.telementalhealthtraining.com/), a private
company that delivers telehealth training and consultation services, offers a website where counselors
can determine whether a particular state has specific regulations on telemental health services, laws
regulating insurance reimbursement, or provisions for out-of-state counselors to practice in that state.
See https://telementalhealthtraining.com/states-rules-and-regulations.
Despite the possible advantages of virtual counseling and the prevalence of its use (Lee et al.,
2023), some mental health professionals believe that virtual counseling services have multiplied
more quickly than the evidence that such services are safe or effective (Welfel, 2016). A number of
concerns have been raised (Froehlich, 2023; Kiriakaki, 2022; Lawson et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2022),
including: (a) the possibility exists that counselors may operate from erroneous assumptions due
to the absence of visual and auditory cues; (b) lack of security of communications could lead to
breaches of confidentiality; (c) a relationship-building human presence between counselor and cli-
ent is lacking; (d) issues that are interpersonal in nature cannot be addressed effectively using this
medium; (e) counselors may not be able to discern when a client’s condition poses a threat to self
or others; (f) counselors may find it difficult to determine an appropriate fee structure for virtual
counseling; (g) the health of counselors may suffer; and (h) protecting clients from incompetent or
unqualified virtual counselors may be difficult. Disadvantages to virtual counseling from the client’s
perspective include the absence of personal warmth (Leibert et al., 2006), frustration with the pace
of online counseling, and problems with using the technology (Haberstroh et al., 2007).
Whether clients’ health insurance providers reimburse for virtual counseling services is an
important consideration. Despite problems obtaining insurance reimbursement prior to the COVID-19
