that emulates real-world interactions (Sacco-Bene et al., 2022). In the future, it will be necessary
to assess the effectiveness of such transformations and to continue to develop teaching practices
specifically tailored to virtual counselor education (Elliott & Henninger, 2020; Li & Su, 2021). The
pace of change is likely to continue to accelerate, presenting new opportunities and challenges for
the transformed process of counselor education.
COUNSELOR EDUCATION PROGRAMS
Informed Consent
Just as clients have the right to know what they are undertaking when they come for counseling,
students have the right to know what will be expected of them before they choose to enter a graduate
program in counseling. In keeping with the spirit of informed consent, prospective students need
enough information to enable them to make wise choices about their graduate studies.
In the American Counseling Association’s Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), §F.8.a. lists quite
explicitly the information that must be provided to prospective students before they enter a program.
This information includes the following:
• The values and ethical principles of the profession
• The type and level of skill and knowledge acquisition required for successful completion of
the training program

-- 392 of 593 --

370 	Section 2 • Issues
• Technology requirements
• Program training goals, objectives, and mission and the subject matter that will be covered
• Bases for evaluation
• Training components that encourage self-growth or self-disclosure as part of the training
process
• The type of supervision settings and the requirements of the sites that are needed to obtain the
required clinical field experiences
• Student and supervisee evaluation and dismissal policies and procedures
• Up-to-date employment prospects for graduates
Application materials, including those found on websites, must include all of these elements and
must be thorough and current. Counselor education programs also have an obligation to ensure that
prospective students can contact a knowledgeable individual at the university if they have questions.
You learned in Chapter 4 that informed consent is a legal as well as an ethical matter. The law
of informed consent requires that individuals be properly informed before their consent to engage in
counseling sessions is valid. Before graduate students can appropriately agree to enter a program into
which they have been accepted and subsequently meet the program’s requirements for graduation,
they must be informed of all the requirements. In addition, the program’s requirements for graduation
must be presented in sufficient detail to allow entering students to fully understand their obligations.
Jorgensen and Brown-Rice (2016) found that 27.7% of CACREP-accredited programs require
applicants to undergo a criminal background check (CBC). Although this may seem intrusive, such a
check can prevent future problems for some applicants. All 50 states require a CBC for school coun-
selors. In 2016 a total of 17 state licensure boards required applicants to pass a CBC (Jorgensen &
Brown-Rice, 2016), and it is likely that all 50 states now require a criminal background check as
part of the counselor licensure application process. Applications for licensure typically ask whether
the applicant has been convicted of a felony. As an informed consent issue, we believe it would be
wise for all programs, whether or not they require a CBC, to inform prospective students of the pos-
sible consequences of any legal violations in their past.
Perhaps the most important goal of informed consent in admissions is to ensure that prospec-
tive students understand that becoming a counselor involves more than just acquiring knowledge.
Counseling students are instead expected to engage in a variety of learning experiences that differ
from those required in most other disciplines (Pease-Carter & Barrio Minton, 2012). Applicants
should be made aware that their training will challenge them on the personal level as well as on the
academic level (Herlihy & Corey, 2015a).
Admissions
Counselor educators strive to admit into training programs individuals who are likely to suc-
ceed at developing the knowledge, skills, and characteristics needed to become effective coun-
selors. Academic ability is one important criterion, and relevant work experience might also be
considered. Some graduate programs list minimum criteria for admission, which might include
a minimum Graduate Record Examination (GRE) score or a Miller Analogy Test (MAT) score, a
minimum undergraduate or graduate grade point average (GPA), or a minimum number of years
of work experience. Swank and Smith-Adcock (2014) found that 70% of counselor education
programs use standardized test scores and that 95% use GPA as a criterion for admission into mas-
ter’s-degree programs. There is some evidence to suggest that undergraduate GPA and GRE scores
are useful for predicting both graduate GPAs and student scores on the Counselor Preparation
Comprehensive Exam (CPCE; Hatchett et al., 2017). Many counselor education programs require

-- 393 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	371
that students pass the CPCE to graduate. Many programs also attempt to gather information by
requiring applicants to submit letters of reference or a personal statement or essay. With the advent
of artificial intelligence, however, counselor educators have become concerned that personal state-
ments may have been written by an avatar. Concerns surrounding artificial intelligence will need to
be addressed on an ongoing basis in admissions processes as new technologies emerge.
Demonstrated academic ability is a relatively straightforward criterion to apply. Admissions deci-
sions become more difficult when we consider that learning to be a counselor involves more than mas-
tery of academic content. Admissions screening also involves an evaluation of the personal qualities and
interpersonal skills of applicants (Garner, 2020). Personal characteristics associated with the ability to
succeed as a counselor include maturity, flexibility, emotional stability (Nagpal & Richie, 2002), accep-
tance, open-mindedness, empathy, warmth, genuineness, and sensitivity (McCaughan & Hill, 2014).
As we noted in Chapter 7 on competence, it is possible for a student to have strong intellectual abilities
and still not possess the personal and interpersonal characteristics to be an effective counselor. Some
individuals who are attracted to careers in counseling seem to have personality or adjustment problems
that would negatively affect their ability to develop the competencies needed to become effective coun-
selors. Therefore, counselor educators attempt to screen applicants carefully and to select only those
students who possess the characteristics associated with counseling effectiveness. As you can imagine,
it is difficult for counselor educators to assess whether program applicants have these personal and
interpersonal attributes. The importance of assessing counselor dispositions has become institutional-
ized in the CACREP accreditation standards (CACREP, 2024); accredited programs are required to
assess the dispositions of their students both at the time of admission and throughout their matriculation
in their respective programs. However, the absence of dispositional measures that reliably predict coun-
selor performance makes this standard difficult to uphold (Hatchett, 2023).
Admissions decisions for applicants are usually based on a review of several factors. These fac-
tors are considered in relation to the number of individuals that the program can accommodate and
the credentials of other applicants. Counseling graduate programs should make it clear that they make
admissions decisions in this manner. University administrators and judges generally would not substi-
tute their judgment for that of faculty members unless it was clear that an applicant had been unfairly
discriminated against or that the faculty had failed to follow the procedures that they had established.
Counselor education programs cannot legally discriminate against prospective students who
are in any category that is protected by the U.S. Constitution or federal statutes and their imple-
menting regulations. These categories include race, sex, disability, age, residence, and alien status
(Kaplin & Lee, 1995; Welfare et al., 2017). Counselor education programs must also be careful to
avoid discriminating against students with disabilities.
In the past, programs have given special consideration to applicants whose characteristics are
underrepresented in the program or in the profession of counseling. Admissions practices are being
scrutinized in the wake of the U.S. Supreme Court decision in Standards for Fair Admissions, Inc. v.
President and Fellows of Harvard College (2023), which prohibited affirmative action practices in
which race was considered in admission decisions. Regardless of developments in the legal arena,
however, nondiscrimination remains an ethical mandate. Counselor educators have an ethical respon-
sibility to ensure that counselor education programs reflect the cultural diversity of our society.
According to the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014):
Counselor educators [should] actively attempt to recruit and retain a diverse student body. Counselor
educators [should] demonstrate commitment to multicultural/diversity competence by recognizing
and valuing diverse cultures and types of abilities students bring to the training experience. Counselor
educators [should] provide appropriate accommodations that enhance and support diverse student
well-being and academic performance. (§F.11.b.)

-- 394 of 593 --

372 	Section 2 • Issues
For some counselor education programs that are located in geographical areas where the
population is not very diverse, recruiting a diverse student body can be a challenge. Even when a
diverse range of students can be attracted to a program, a recursive situation may occur in which
a program continues to admit and then ultimately lose students who are members of marginalized
societal groups because the students are always entering programs in which most of the students are
members of the dominant culture (Behl et al., 2017; Avent Harris et al., 2017). Hipolito-Delgado
et al. (2021), after interviewing 19 counseling graduate students of color, discovered that the par-
ticipants’ motivations for attending graduate school included their interest in serving marginalized
communities, their ability to find programs committed to diversity, and the support they received
during the application process to graduate school. Some programs now use flexible admissions
criteria to increase student diversity rather than continue to rely on GPA and GRE scores, which are
less reliable predictors of success for racially and ethnically diverse applicants. Hannon et al. (2023)
have recommended that counselor education programs increase the racial or ethnic diversity of their
applicant pools by removing standardized test requirements (e.g., the Graduate Record Exam) for
admission. Hammonds and Perguson (2022) have advocated for a holistic admissions process that
includes evaluation of applicants’ life experiences, academic metrics, and attributes or dispositions.
Curriculum Issues
In Chapter 2, we discussed in some detail the curricular components that a counselor education
program must contain to be accredited by the Council for Accreditation of Counseling and Related
Educational Programs (CACREP). CACREP standards (CACREP, 2024) are generally accepted by
the profession as a model curriculum for master’s-level preparation of counselors. Here, we high-
light some of the ethical responsibilities of counselor educators in delivering the curriculum and
explore one of the most ethically sensitive components of training—self-growth experiences.
TEACHING ETHICS, MULTICULTURAL COUNSELING, AND SOCIAL JUSTICE 	Counselor edu-
cators must make students aware of the ethical standards of the counseling profession and their
responsibilities to the profession (ACA, 2014, §F.7.e.). Historically, in some counselor education
programs, ethics was taught as a separate course, whereas it was infused into existing courses in
other programs. Today, best practice is for ethical and legal issues to be both taught as a discrete
course and infused throughout the training process when students are at various developmental
levels. Students who are just beginning their studies in this area may look at ethical issues one way.
Later, as they acquire direct experience with clients during their practicum and internship, their
views are likely to evolve.
Ethical decision-making skills are essential; experts polled by Herlihy and Dufrene (2011)
identified helping students develop these skills as the most important ethical issue in counselor
preparation. Ametrano (2014) has described how an ethics course can teach ethical decision mak-
ing, increase tolerance for ambiguity, and enhance student awareness of how values influence
decision making in the wake of the Ward v. Wilbanks (2010) legal case (described in detail in
Chapter 3).
Counselor educators have strengthened their focus on multicultural pedagogy in recent decades
(Hall et al., 2014) in response to a growing recognition that multicultural competence is essential to
ethical practice. As was noted in Chapter 3, it is almost certain that counselors will encounter clients
whose cultural backgrounds are different from their own, which presents a challenge for counselor
educators who must determine how best to prepare multiculturally competent counselors. Counselor
educators are thus obligated to “infuse material related to multicultural/diversity into all courses and
workshops for the development of professional counselors” (ACA, 2014, §F.7.c.).

-- 395 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	373
An often-overlooked resource is the large numbers of international students in counselor educa-
tion programs, who can offer diverse perspectives on curricula that may be Eurocentric (Kuo et al.,
2018). In addition, counselor educators must infuse multicultural/diversity competency in their training
and supervision practices and “train students to gain awareness, knowledge, and skills in the compe-
tencies of multicultural practice” (ACA, 2014, §F.11.c.). Despite these mandates, some students find
it difficult to remain open to multicultural course content and react with reluctance, attitudinal rigidity,
and entrenchment of prejudiced beliefs (Malott et al., 2023); and counselor educators have invested
considerable effort in determining the most effective approaches to increase these students’ receptivity.
As a result, multicultural counseling courses often are taught using a combination of didactic, experien-
tial, and community service learning approaches. Killian and Florin (2020) found that approaches that
enforced both direct exposure and self-reflection (rather than passive knowledge attainment) were best
for facilitating the development of students’ multicultural competence.
Social justice, a growing force within the counseling profession, has created a shift in how
prospective counselors are prepared for the profession, and counselor education programs are
infusing issues related to multiculturalism and social justice into all counselor education courses.
Students often believe that they are inadequately prepared in social justice and advocacy, and
counselor educators are responding to this belief by implementing pedagogical strategies such
as exploring the dynamics of privilege and oppression, engaging students in the development of
critical consciousness, using experiential activities, infusing social justice advocacy into supervi-
sion (Bevly et al., 2017), and engaging students in service learning projects to shift learning to
beyond the classroom (Manis, 2012; Steele et al., 2014). Counselor educators are teaching students
the skills involved in broaching (Day-Vines et al., 2021) the topics of race, ethnicity, and culture
so that social justice issues are addressed in the counseling relationship. Singh et al. (2020) have
noted that although numerous improvements were made in the attention given to multicultural,
social justice, and advocacy issues in the 2014 ACA Code of Ethics, ethical practice entails more
than adherence to the Code, and the spirit of social justice must be infused throughout all compo-
nents of counselor training.
TEACHING THEORIES AND TECHNIQUES 	Counselor educators have an ethical responsibility to
teach current theories and techniques that are appropriate to the populations that students will be coun-
seling. This obligation is articulated in the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), which states that counselor
educators should “provide instruction based on current information and knowledge available in the
profession” (§F.7.b.). This means that counselor education programs must expose students to a wide
range of counseling theories and encourage students to develop their own theoretical stances. Before
the 1960s, models of counseling that were taught were almost exclusively those developed by Euro-
American males. It was generally assumed by counselor educators that these models had universal
application. However, today’s counselor educators are aware that traditionally taught theories may
be inadequate to address the needs of diverse clientele. They also emphasize multicultural, feminist,
relational–cultural, ecological, and other systemic approaches that may be more applicable to diverse
client populations.
Counselor educators should “promote the use of techniques/procedures that are grounded in
theory and/or have an empirical or scientific foundation” (ACA, 2014, §F.7.h.). When counselor
educators introduce students to techniques or modalities that are developing or innovative, they
must explain to students the potential risks and ethical considerations of using those techniques or
modalities. The intent of this standard is not to discourage innovation; rather, it serves as a reminder
of the importance of intentionality in selecting techniques and as a caution against implementing
techniques that may be risky to use with clients if those techniques are not well proven.

-- 396 of 593 --

374 	Section 2 • Issues
EXPERIENTIAL LEARNING 	Counselor education programs, whether they are offered in a tradi-
tional or an online format, must integrate academic study and supervised practice (ACA, 2014,
§F.7.d.). Typically, training programs present students with a graduated series of opportunities to
practice the skills they are learning, beginning with an introductory skills or techniques course and
progressing through a supervised practicum and internship.
A particular type of experiential learning that occurs in counselor education programs involves
self-growth experiences in which students increase their understanding of interpersonal dynamics
and their awareness of self and others. An ethical concern regarding self-growth experiences is how
counselor educators can balance students’ rights to privacy and the need to provide effective train-
ing. Until the mid-1980s, professors who had any administrative, supervisory, or evaluative author-
ity over students were prohibited from offering students self-growth experiences, and counselor
education programs were required to offer students alternatives to self-growth experiences without
penalty or prejudice. These guidelines, which certainly protected students’ privacy rights, allowed
for the possibility that a student could go through an entire training program without participating in
a self-growth experience. In recent decades, there has been a shift in thinking regarding self-growth
experiences. The current Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) clarifies that self-growth is an expected com-
ponent of counselor training. Standard F.8.c. states that counselor educators should be “mindful
of ethical principles when they require students to engage in self-growth experiences.” Counselor
educators should therefore inform students that they have a right to decide what information about
themselves they will share or withhold in class. Evaluation has to be independent of the student’s
self-disclosure—that is, students cannot be graded on their level of self-disclosure. In addition,
counselor educators may require students to seek professional help to address any personal con-
cerns that have the potential to affect their professional competence (§F.8.d.). It can be uncomfort-
able for students to confront their own biases and prejudices that may emerge in group discussions,
so it is important for students to understand that a rationale exists for requiring such experiences.
As was discussed in Chapter 3, students who are unaware of their own countertransference issues or
biases are at risk of providing inadequate or even harmful services to clients.
DEVELOPING AND EVALUATING COUNSELING SKILLS 	Effective counseling requires coun-
selors to apply techniques and behaviors that are accepted as effective in the professional litera-
ture (Schmidt, 2016). In addition, they must know when to engage in which behaviors and how
to acknowledge the unique personalities of the counselor and the client in the counseling process.
Counseling includes scientific principles that are artfully applied. Students may be uneasy about
being evaluated on their skill development because these criteria are less objective than those in
tests of academic performance that use scores or numbers.
Counselor educators who teach counseling skills must define effective counseling in specific
and concrete terms so that students can become proficient in their skill development. Students who
are not performing well in counseling skills classes have the rights to be made aware of their defi-
ciencies and to receive recommended steps to improve their performance.
If an evaluation or grade is challenged, counselor educators have to explain to peers, and later
perhaps to a judge or jury, the criteria they used to evaluate a student’s counseling skills. Counselor
educators are obligated to:
• Indicate the skills to be learned in the course in terms that are as specific and behavioral as
possible.
• Give periodic, specific feedback regarding the development of those skills.
• Have a method of transferring evaluations into numbers or grades that can be justified.

-- 397 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	375
14-1 The Case of Naomi
Naomi is a first-semester master’s-degree student in a counselor education program. Over the course
of the semester, she does excellent work in her classes in counseling theories and human growth
and development, earning an A in both classes. Naomi is also enrolled in the beginning counsel-
ing techniques class. During the first half of the semester, she is evaluated twice by the instructor
on her skill development as demonstrated in role-play counseling sessions with classmates. After
she receives a grade of unacceptable on her first evaluation, she makes an appointment with the
professor, who gives her specific feedback regarding the areas of performance in which she needs
to improve. At midterm, she shows very little improvement, and the instructor asks her to make
another appointment to receive individualized feedback. Naomi does not follow up on this request.
After observing Naomi’s counseling performance for a third time, the instructor takes her aside after
class and explains that Naomi is at risk of failing the course if she continues to be unable to demon-
strate the basic active-listening skills required of counselors. Together, they develop a growth plan
with specific behavioral objectives. For the remainder of the semester, Naomi is so anxious about
performance evaluations that she freezes up and makes no progress. She also ignores the instruc-
tor’s suggestion that she seek counseling to deal with her performance anxiety. At the end of the
semester, she does not receive a passing grade, which means, by program policy, that she cannot
continue in the program. Naomi files an appeal, asserting that she is an excellent student, as evi-
denced by her A grades in her other two courses.
• Why do you think a graduate student in counseling, such as Naomi, might get A’s in two
courses but a failing grade in her counseling techniques course?
• What might Naomi have done differently to improve her chances of receiving a passing
grade?
Discussion: Naomi has very little chance of succeeding with her appeal. She received feedback
throughout the semester and was given specific instructions regarding what she needed to do to
improve her performance. In addition, she failed to respond to the instructor’s requests to make a
second appointment or to take steps to deal with her performance anxiety. There is no evidence
that the instructor violated procedures or was unfair in evaluating Naomi. This case illustrates the
effort required to evaluate students on their skill development. This course instructor devoted
much more time and effort in working with Naomi than would have been required had the course
been a traditional, didactic course in which grades were based on performance on objective tests.
We would hope that Naomi seeks personal counseling to learn to cope with her anxiety if she
wishes to continue in her graduate program. She will be expected to demonstrate her skills if she
repeats the class and to continue to do so in other courses throughout the program.
Evaluation issues can be very complex for educators who teach skills courses, particularly for
those who teach group counseling courses. Among counselor educators, the consensus is that stu-
dents learn best when didactic instruction is accompanied by experiential learning about group pro-
cess and dynamics. Generally, the course instructor avoids serving as the facilitator of the experiential
component of the class because this dual role creates ethical dilemmas when evaluating students for
continuation in the program. Although students are informed about the limits of confidentiality at
the outset of the group experience, a level of trust can develop in the group that fosters personal risk
taking. Student self-disclosures may allow course instructors to have knowledge about a student that

-- 398 of 593 --

376 	Section 2 • Issues
might cause educators to have concerns about the student’s fitness for the profession. The counselor
educators would then face the dilemma of not wanting to violate the student’s confidentiality but also
feeling the need to fulfill their gatekeeper role. Because of these dual-role conflicts, counselor educa-
tion programs have developed several alternative methods for delivering the experiential component
of a group counseling class. Some of these alternative methods include having advanced students or
counselors from the community lead the groups, having the component led by adjunct instructors
who have no ongoing evaluative role with students as they progress through the program, having
actors play the roles of group members (Fall & Levitov, 2002), sending students off campus to par-
ticipate in group experiences, using videos to engage students in observational learning, or using a
fishbowl technique to combine observational and experiential learning (Spargo et al., 2010). During
the COVID-19 pandemic, most of these alternatives were unavailable. Urkmez et al. (2021) studied a
group of students who began the group experience in a face-to-face format and abruptly had to tran-
sition to an online format because of COVID-19. Although the students reported a reasonably good
experience with online groups, including comfort and cohesiveness with the other group members,
most voiced a preference for face-to-face sessions. Nonetheless, we anticipate that virtual group
counseling courses will remain an option for students. Wathen et al.
(2021) have offered a model that considers the ethical challenges as well
as the adaptations that are needed to ensure success with these courses.
EVALUATIONS OF FIELD EXPERIENCE PERFORMANCE 	Just as evaluat-
ing the development of counseling skills can be difficult, evaluating students’
performance in their practicum and internship experiences is challenging
(Cook et al., 2018; Storlie et al., 2017). It is important for counselor educa-
tion programs to have specific and understandable procedures for students to
enter into and complete field experiences. Developing practicum and intern-
ship sites, communicating with on-site supervisors, and orienting everyone
involved are complex tasks for counselor educators, yet these responsibili-
ties are clearly delineated in the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014). According
to §F.7.i., counselor educators must develop clear policies regarding field
placement and other clinical experiences, and they must provide clearly
stated roles and responsibilities for students, site supervisors, and program
supervisors. Counselor educators must also confirm that site supervisors are
qualified to provide supervision and are informed of their ethical and profes-
sional responsibilities as supervisors.
During field experiences, graduate students are evaluated by a
number of professors and on-site supervisors. If a student’s performance
is evaluated negatively, counselor educators must ensure that the evalu-
ations are fair. To prevent having negative evaluations successfully chal-
lenged, counselor educators must follow all the procedures that have
been established by the program for field placements.
PERSONAL AND PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT 	Because there is a well-established relation-
ship between clinical competence and personal and professional development, counselor educators
have an ethical obligation to evaluate student development in areas other than academic perfor-
mance and skills acquisition. CACREP accreditation standards (2024) require counselor educators
to assess students’ professional and personal growth and development. Characteristics that have
been deemed to be crucial to personal and professional development are discussed in Chapter 7.

-- 399 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	377
When student development proceeds according to faculty expectations, problems are
unlikely to arise. However, sometimes students struggle in their personal and professional
development (Lundquist et al., 2020; Um & Bardhoshi, 2022). Historically, counselor educa-
tors have shown a preference for using informal means to address these struggles (Foster &
McAdams, 2009). Counselor educators are aware that a great deal of subjectivity is involved
in such evaluations and may be reluctant to negatively evaluate students along these dimen-
sions because of concern about being unfair, a hope that the struggles will self-correct with
further training, or fear that the students will bring lawsuits against them (Gaubatz & Vera,
2002). Despite this possible reluctance, however, counselor educators recognize the needs to be
clear and specific when evaluating personal and professional development and to communicate
their expectations to students. Most programs have developed written assessment forms that
include criteria for personal and professional growth. Foster and McAdams (2009) have noted
that these types of evaluation can be anxiety provoking for stu-
dents and that students who are not familiar with the programs’
assessment policies and procedures may consider the policies
to be punitive.
Classmates often are aware of peers who are having prob-
lems with professional competency. Results of one study sug-
gested that 95% of peers had been personally affected by this
situation (Rosenberg et al., 2005), which indicates that the learn-
ing of all students is disrupted by these problems. Brown-Rice
and Furr (2019) found that approximately two-thirds of doctoral
students were aware of peers with problems with professional
competency. These students reported that they were affected
negatively by being in a program with these peers and were frus-
trated with educators for allowing problematic peers to continue
their doctoral training. Generally, students understand the impor-
tance of intervention for the sake of the classmate’s future clients
as well as for the profession (Foster et al., 2014), but they may be
uncertain as to whether or how they should take action. Brown-
Rice and Furr (2013) and Parker et al. (2014) have recommended
that counselor educators develop, implement, and inform stu-
dents about formal procedures that provide an opportunity for
students to approach a faculty member when a peer is struggling.
14-2 The Case of Nasima
Nasima is a second-year master’s-degree student who has just begun her practicum experience,
counseling clients in a community agency. As Nasima has progressed through her degree pro-
gram, she has become friends with a group of four other students who entered the program at
the same time Nasima did. They have all remained friends and socialize together. One of these
friends, Tonya, is enrolled in practicum at the same agency. Tonya has confided in Nasima that she
is finding her work with clients to be very stressful, and Nasima has noticed that Tonya is becoming
intoxicated when the two of them go out socializing with their friends. Tonya has also confided that
she is struggling as a counselor and is not selecting the videotapes of her bad counseling sessions

-- 400 of 593 --

378 	Section 2 • Issues
to share with her supervisor. Nasima is concerned about Tonya and is worried that Tonya’s clients
are not being well served. At the same time, Nasima doesn’t want to take any action that could
“get Tonya in trouble with her supervisor.” Nasima doesn’t know what to do.
• Does Nasima have an ethical obligation to take some action to protect Tonya’s clients?
• What do you think Nasima should do in this situation?
Discussion: According to the ACA Code of Ethics (2014), Nasima as a student has the same
ethical obligations as does a counselor who is already in practice. She cannot ignore the pos-
sibilities that Tonya may be in danger of becoming impaired and may not be providing adequate
counseling services to clients. Nasima’s desire to protect her friend is understandable, and her
first step should be to talk with Tonya about her concerns. She should then encourage Tonya to
seek personal counseling to deal with her stress and excessive drinking and to inform her super-
visor of Tonya’s struggles. If Tonya is not open to hearing Nasima’s concerns, then Nasima should
let Tonya know that she will need to speak with the supervisor herself. She can emphasize that
she will be doing this out of concern for the well-being of both Tonya and Tonya’s clients and in
fulfillment of her ethical obligations.
Gatekeeping
As we noted in Chapter 7, competence in counseling is hard to define. Nonetheless, counselor
educators are gatekeepers to the profession and must evaluate students to determine whether they
should be allowed to progress through and graduate from their counselor preparation programs
(Surette & Brenton, 2022). It would be unfair to future clients if incompetent or unprepared stu-
dents were allowed to graduate from these programs. At the same time, it would be unfair to stu-
dents if this evaluation process were not ongoing, systematic, and conscientiously communicated.
Therefore, counselor educators must clearly state to students the level of competency expected,
their appraisal methods, and the timing of evaluations (ACA, 2014, §F.9.a.; Lambie & Haugen,
2022). They should provide students with ongoing feedback regarding their performance through-
out the training program.
Counselor educators share a strong commitment to giving students every possible opportunity
to succeed in their studies and to keeping students informed of their progress. They hope that stu-
dents will use this feedback to build on their strengths and to remediate problem areas or deficiencies.
Sometimes, however, students are not able to achieve the needed counseling competencies
(Prikhidko & Swank, 2018). When this happens, counselor educators have an ethical obligation
to exercise their gatekeeping responsibilities. Gatekeeping is a process of evaluation and reme-
diation to ensure compliance with accreditation competencies and ethical standards (DeDiego &
Burgin, 2016). The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) advises counselor educators to (a) assist students
in securing remediation, (b) seek consultation from other professionals and document their decision
to dismiss or refer students for assistance, and (c) ensure that students know in a timely manner that
problems exist and provide them with due process (§F.9.b.).
Gatekeeping has been discussed extensively in the professional literature in the years since a
counseling student was dismissed from a public university in 2005 on the grounds of deficient pro-
fessional performance. The student brought a lawsuit against the counseling program faculty and the
university (Plaintiff v. Rector and Board of Visitors of The College of William and Mary, 2005). The
lawsuits brought by dismissed students in Ward v. Wilbanks (2010) and Keeton v. Anderson-Wiley,

-- 401 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	379
et al. (2011) brought the issue of values conflicts into bold relief (Burkholder et al., 2014) and have
generated recommendations for working with students whose personal values conflict with those of
the counseling profession (e.g., Bayne et al., 2022; Taylor Rouse & Doughty Horn, 2022).
Gate-slipping occurs when counselor educators or supervisors recognize that a student is hav-
ing problems with professional performance but do not intervene. This may occur because of a lack
of clear evidence of student deficiencies, a bias toward leniency, a fear of recrimination (including
a lawsuit), a fear of appearing to be culturally insensitive, a choice to step behind the empathy veil
(a term coined by Brown-Rice and Furr [2014]) to describe the counselor educator’s need to empa-
thize with students), or a lack of support from colleagues or administrators (Brown-Rice & Furr,
2016; DeDiego & Bergin, 2016; Jorgensen & Brown-Rice, 2016).
Doctoral students often are involved in the process of gatekeeping with master’s-level stu-
dents. Charnley (2021), Corley et al. (2020), and Smarinsky et al. (2023) all found that doctoral
students felt unprepared for gatekeeping roles and responsibilities. Counselor education programs
would thus be wise to incorporate specific training in gatekeeping into their doctoral curriculums
(DeCino et al., 2022; Freeman et al., 2020; Rapp et al., 2018). The doctoral student participants in
Charnley’s (2021) study unanimously expressed that although gatekeeping was addressed at some
point in their curriculum, it was not addressed sufficiently or explicitly enough in their classes.
Gilbert et al. (2019) raised the concern that gatekeeping in online programs has not been
researched. Sheperis et al. (2020a, 2020b) noted that gatekeeping has become much more complex
with the advent of virtual counselor education, raising new questions that need to be addressed. For
example, to what extent can a counselor educator or supervisor in one location monitor the behavior
of a student in an entirely different location?
REMEDIATION 	The focus of the gatekeeping literature was on dismissing students rather than on
remediation (Henderson & Dufrene, 2011) until recent years, when the remediation process began
to receive more attention. Dufrene and Henderson (2009) defined remediation as “a documented,
procedural process that addresses perceived inabilities in trainees’ performance with the intent to pro-
vide trainees with specific means to remedy their inabilities” (p. 151). McAdams and Foster (2007)
described remediation procedures that were developed by the counselor education faculty at the
College of William and Mary as an outgrowth of the lawsuit that was filed against them. In addition,
Dufrene and Henderson (2009) and Kress and Protivnak (2009) have described the steps involved in
developing and implementing an individualized remediation plan or a professional development plan
that is a behaviorally focused contract between the faculty and the student. Teixeira (2017) found
that the remediation interventions used most frequently are discussing the problem with the student
and starting due process procedures. Researchers have also identified a myriad of other strategies for
remediation intervention, such as referring a student for personal counseling, increasing or changing
supervision of the student, having the student repeat coursework, tutoring, assigning a peer mentor,
increasing observation of clinical work, assigning or recommending readings and workshops, writing
a letter of concern, and restricting some form of student participation in the program (e.g., a leave
of absence) (Henderson & Dufrene, 2011; Teixeira, 2017). Remediation continues to be a problem-
atic issue, however (Rouse & Horn, 2022). Although personal counseling may be recommended
or required, no studies have demonstrated the effects of required counseling on the professional
performance of remediated students (Byne & Shufelt, 2014; Prosek et al., 2013). Further, Brown
(2013) found that counselor education programs are inconsistent in the terms they use to describe
problematic student behavior and that programs continue to rely on subjective means to assess such
problems. Rust et al. (2013) also found a lack of standardization across programs and noted that
little is known about how cultural differences may factor into how problematic behavior is defined.

-- 402 of 593 --

380 	Section 2 • Issues
These researchers recommended that counselor education programs develop formal assessment
tools, including qualitative and quantitative methods, and publish specific criteria for assessing prob-
lematic behaviors. Although there remains a lack of consensus among counselor educators regarding
the most effective approach to remediation (Salpietro et al., 2021), some evidence exists to sug-
gest that remediation processes are improving (Guiffrida et al., 2019). Salpietro et al. (2022) found
that counselor educators are using developmentally appropriate, individualized, and strengths-based
approaches and perceive them to be effective.
An issue that has been addressed only rarely in the literature is the need for counselor educators
to recognize their gatekeeping responsibilities with respect to colleagues. Brown-Rice and Furr (2015)
found that 75% of the counselor educators they surveyed had observed colleagues who displayed
problems with professional competence and were concerned about how students were being affected.
DISMISSAL DECISIONS 	The remediation process does not always work as desired. Sometimes,
even after counselor educators have identified problems and have assisted a student in securing
remedial assistance, the student is still unable to demonstrate mastery of the required skills, knowl-
edge, or dispositions. When efforts at remediation fail, counselor educators are obligated to dismiss
from the preparation program students who are unable to provide competent counseling services.
In other disciplines in which students must master skills, such as medicine and nursing, courts
defer to the judgment of professors, unless established procedures are violated or professors are
clearly unfair in rendering their evaluations (Connelly v. University of Vermont, 1965; Dutile, 2001).
In Connelly, the judges explained that courts defer to professors on academic matters because, in mat-
ters of scholarship, the school authorities are uniquely qualified by training and experience to judge
the qualifications of a student, and efficiency of instruction depends in no small degree on the school
faculty’s freedom from interference from other noneducational tribunals. It is only when the school
authorities abuse this discretion that a court may interfere with the decision to dismiss a student.
The U.S. Supreme Court case Horowitz v. Board of Curators of the University of Missouri
(1978) has established that professional programs have the right to dismiss students who have excel-
lent academic records but poor performance in applied areas. In Horowitz, a medical student who
had excellent grades on written examinations was dismissed for deficiencies in the areas of clinical
performance, peer and patient relations, and personal hygiene.
In the realm of counselor education, a student at the College of William and Mary brought
suit against the university and the counselor education faculty who dismissed her. McAdams and
Foster (2007) and McAdams et al. (2007) have described in detail the lengthy legal process that
ensued, which resulted in a decision in favor of the counseling program faculty and university.
These authors concluded that dismissal decisions in counselor education programs are likely to be
legally justified but only after faculty have provided a student with fair and just opportunities to
remediate any identified deficiencies.
As was discussed in Chapter 3, lawsuits were brought by students who were dismissed from
their counseling graduate programs at Eastern Michigan University and Augusta State University in
Georgia. The faculty at those institutions determined that the two students were deficient in multicul-
tural competence after the students refused to counsel LGBTGEQIAP+ clients regarding same-sex
relationships. Both students were dismissed only after they had failed to meet the requirements of the
remediation plans offered to them. These cases are likely to have had an impact on the criteria and
processes by which dismissal decisions are made. Hutchens et al. (2013) concluded that the decisions
in Ward and Keeton reinforce the importance of counselor education programs ensuring that all fac-
ulty members agree on policies and on how they should be implemented and that policies and proce-
dures should be applied uniformly to all students, well documented, and readily available to students.

-- 403 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	381
Dugger and Francis (2014) reflected on the lessons they learned from their involvement as defendants
in Ward v. Wilbanks. They reiterated the importance of having and adhering to policies and procedures
for dismissal decisions and noted that it is also vital to have a concrete process for responding to a
lawsuit, to assume all communications will be subpoenaed, and to seek support throughout the process.
Counselor educators never take lightly the decision to dismiss a student from a preparation
program. Gatekeeping is an emotionally intense experience for counselor educators, who often
experience dissonance, discomfort, guilt, anger, and role confusion as well as empathy, compassion,
and sensitivity for students (DeCino et al., 2020). Counselor educators have identified only a few
behaviors that are likely to be irremediable; these include predatory behavior, breaches of confi-
dentiality, substance abuse, intentional ethical violations, sexual harassment, serious and chronic
mental health issues, and deliberate cheating (Schuermann et al., 2018). Harrichand et al. (2022)
noted the difficulties inherent in balancing the need to create psychological safety for learners while
simultaneously monitoring for gatekeeping purposes.
Counseling graduate students who are politically conservative or deeply religious sometimes
feel uncomfortable in the environment of counselor education programs, which tend to be socially lib-
eral. For counseling graduate students to learn and thrive in a counselor education graduate program,
they must feel psychologically safe. Counselor educators have power over the students they teach, and
students need to be sure that the power professors have over them will not be abused. Giordano et al.
(2018) surveyed 264 graduate students from four different university counseling graduate programs.
Students were asked to complete an instrument that measured their perceptions of psychological
safety and their perceptions of their professors’ appreciation of differences. Older students and more
politically conservative students in this study perceived less psychological safety and less appreciation
of differences in their training programs. Results from studies such as this one challenge counselor
educators to examine their biases and behaviors toward older and more politically conservative stu-
dents. University professors tend to be liberal in their beliefs (Yancey, 2011), so they must be careful to
prevent imposing their personal beliefs on students whose beliefs differ from their own.
The care that is taken in dismissal decisions is reflected in the ethical standards, which state
that counselor educators must use ongoing evaluation to become aware of and address the inability
of some students to achieve counseling competencies (ACA, 2014, §F.9.b.). When limitations are
identified, counselor educators should seek professional consultation and document their decisions
to refer students for assistance or dismiss them from the program. Students must also have recourse
to address such decisions and be provided with due process (§F.9.b.). The CACREP standards also
address the issue of dismissal of students for nonacademic reasons. These standards require that
when evaluations indicate that a student is inappropriate for a counseling program, faculty should
assist in facilitating the student’s transition out of the program and into a more appropriate area of
study, if that is possible (CACREP, 2024). The case of Naomi in this chapter is a good example of
how a student can be academically competent but deficient in counseling skill development. The
program faculty can assist Naomi, if she is willing, to find a different area of graduate study that
may be more compatible with her competencies.
No hearing is required if a student is dismissed for academic reasons. However, if a stu-
dent is dismissed for misconduct, a hearing is required. It is vital that the student’s rights to due
process are observed. This process involves meeting with the student to clearly communicate
the concerns, providing the student with sufficient information to make needed changes, and
creating a remediation plan (Kimball et al., 2019). It is important for faculty members to avoid
mixing misconduct charges with academic or performance reasons when they discipline or dis-
miss students (Horowitz v. Board of Curators of the University of Missouri, 1978; Regents of the
University of Michigan v. Ewing, 1985).

-- 404 of 593 --

382 	Section 2 • Issues
ENDORSEMENT 	Professional preparation programs such as medicine, nursing, and counseling
have a responsibility to society to ensure not only that graduates have the knowledge necessary to
practice successfully but also that they have the ability to apply that knowledge in a manner that
leads to effective professional practice.
Although unfavorable decisions or recommendations can be difficult to make, counselor edu-
cators should not endorse a student for employment or for any credential (such as a graduate degree,
certification, or licensure) if they believe that the student is not qualified (ACA, 2014, §F.6.d.).
Counselor educators, as gatekeepers to the profession, have responsibilities not only to their stu-
dents but also to the future clients of their students and to the profession as a whole. Typically, coun-
selor educators decline to provide a recommendation or endorsement when their candid assessment
would not be helpful to the student.
FACULTY AND STUDENT ISSUES
Faculty Competence
Competence is an issue that applies to counselor educators as well as to students. Counselor educa-
tors must also be able to provide services competently. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014, §F.7.a.)
requires counselor educators to be skilled as teachers and practitioners. They must be knowledgeable
regarding the ethical, legal, and regulatory aspects of the profession, and they have an obligation to
make students aware of their responsibilities. They must also conduct counselor education programs
in an ethical manner and serve as role models for the profession.
As role models, counselor educators must practice what they preach and make ongoing efforts
to ensure their own continuing competence. Reading and attending workshops and conferences to
stay up to date on developments in the field, working a few hours a week at a counseling agency in
the community to keep their skills as practitioners sharp, paying serious attention to student evalua-
tions of their teaching and courses and implementing recommended improvements, and conducting
and publishing their own research are just a few of the strategies counselor educators might use to
keep themselves on their growing edge.
Standard F.7.b., a new requirement in the most recent Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014), addresses
counselor educator competence directly. This standard requires counselor educators to provide
instruction that is based on the current information and knowledge available to the profession and
is within their areas of knowledge and competence. Some counselor education programs may have
a practice of allowing the most experienced professors to choose the courses they want to teach,
which leaves new and inexperienced faculty members teaching the courses that no one else wants to
teach (Kaplan & Martz, 2014b). Continuing this practice could put new professors in the position of
being in violation of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014).
Counselor educators have been required to develop new competencies in the technology
arena in response to the switch to virtual learning necessitated by the COVID-19 lockdown. As
Okoye et al. (2021) have noted, technology-mediated education has become a fundamental part of
modern-day teaching and learning in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic; for this reason,
counselor educators must continue to learn to manage ever-evolving technologies to improve
student learning outcomes.
Diversity Considerations
Counselor education programs have an ethical responsibility to recruit and retain not only cultur-
ally diverse students but also culturally diverse faculty (ACA, 2014, §F.11.a., §F.11.b.). Although
Schweiger et al. (2012) observed a trend toward attempting to hire faculty who are diverse both

-- 405 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	383
theoretically and culturally to give students a more varied set of perspectives, the counselor educa-
tion professorate remains predominantly White. Avent Harris et al. (2019) reported that women fac-
ulty members continue to experience microaggressions (subtle negative messages). Although women
are well represented in the ranks of counselor educators, Hill et al. (2005) found that female coun-
selor educators’ life satisfaction was negatively influenced by toxic faculty environments, office poli-
tics, and being overcontrolled by others, as well as by a lack of mentors. Mothers who are counselor
educators have been found to have more job satisfaction if they have support from colleagues, do
not have children under age 6, and do not work at research universities (Neale-McFall et al., 2018).
Asian American counselor educators continue to experience distinct challenges, compared to the chal-
lenges that their White counterparts experience (Litam & Chan, 2021), and these challenges may have
increased at the outset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Foreign-born faculty members interviewed by
Interiano-Shiverdecker et al. (2022) believed that their cultural differences were a strength but that
rejection of their cultural differences presented barriers to connection and sometimes a reason for
discrimination. For these faculty, the COVID-19 pandemic was an added stressor as it greatly limited
their ability to travel and visit family and friends living abroad. Counselor education programs must
therefore address racism and sexism, develop and implement strategies to increase the number of
ethnic/racial minority faculty, and facilitate the progress of minority and women faculty through the
ranks (Newhart et al., 2020). Thacker et al. (2021) have called upon faculty members with privileged
identities to examine how their roles and behaviors influence the experiences of colleagues who do not
share their privilege. Until such efforts are successful, there will continue to be a dearth of mentors and
role models for racial or ethnic minority students who aspire to become counselor educators.
Student–Faculty Research Collaboration
Counselor educators are often subject to the same pressures felt by their colleagues in other
disciplines when they work in a publish or perish environment. These pressures may tempt
counselor educators to become overly concerned about the number of their publications and to
neglect considerations of quality or their actual contributions to a study (Borders et al., 2019;
Hatchett et al., 2020; Welfel, 2016).
14-3 The Case of Lakiesha
Lakiesha was elated to be accepted into a doctoral program in counselor education. During her
first semester she worked very hard at her studies. Dr. Wilks, one of their professors, was particu-
larly impressed with one of the papers Lakiesha had written and suggested that Lakiesha consider
publishing it. Lakiesha made several attempts to seek out Dr. Wilks for guidance on how to publish,
but he was always too busy working on his own research and writing, so Lakiesha let the matter
drop. Over the next 2 years, Lakiesha continued to be a diligent student and found other profes-
sors to mentor her. She completed her formal coursework and was preparing to sit for the general
examination that, once passed, would admit her into “all but dissertation (ABD)” status. Dr. Wilks
was one of the professors on her general examination committee. Lakiesha was shocked and angry
when she came across a published article by Dr. Wilks that was based largely on the paper Lakiesha
had written for him 2 years ago. Lakiesha wanted to confront him but was afraid that such an action
might negatively affect Dr. Wilks’s evaluation of her examination. She thought about talking to one
of the professors who had been a helpful mentor but was reluctant to put that professor in an awk-
ward position. In the end, Lakiesha didn’t say anything to anyone.

-- 406 of 593 --

384 	Section 2 • Issues
To prevent misunderstandings, it is wise for a student and a professor who wish to undertake a col-
laborative research project to carefully work out, in advance, the arrangements regarding their respective
contributions, deadlines, and publication credit for the completed work (§G.5.e.). Ethically conscientious
counselor educators are careful to avoid exploiting their students who may be eager to contribute to a
professor’s research study or who may feel honored to be asked to assist (Sackett et al., 2015).
Relationships Boundaries Between Counselor
Educators and Students
Counselor educators are obligated to clearly define and maintain ethical, professional, and social
relationship boundaries with their students. They should not seek to meet their personal needs
for affiliation, friendship, or sex through relationships with students. In Chapter 9 we examined
issues surrounding dual relationships between counselors and clients. The relationships between
counselor educators and students are both similar to and different from the relationships between
counselors and clients.
A similarity is that both dyads involve a power differential in an inherently unequal relationship
in which one person is seeking a service: counseling or education. Throughout a graduate-degree
program, and even after a degree is earned, faculty members must evaluate students. Evaluative
responsibilities create a power differential. The power of educators goes far beyond grades:
Counselor educators can provide students with introductions that create networking opportunities;
sponsorship to professional associations; opportunities for research experience and publications
(Ghoston et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2022); and letters of recommendation for scholarships, assistant-
ships, internships, and jobs.
However, faculty–student relationships are different from counselor–client relationships in
some significant ways. Faculty–student relationships are usually characterized by multiple and
overlapping roles (Dickens et al., 2016). The roles in the relationship between counselor and client
are fixed in that the client never becomes the counselor, nor does the counselor ever become the cli-
ent. By contrast, the roles in the educator–student relationship are always in transition as students
progress through the program, first as beginning students, next as advanced students and interns,
• What might have been a good course of action for Lakiesha to take to address this situation?
• Can you think of any justifications that Dr. Wilks might have had for his actions?
Discussion: It appears that Dr. Wilks may have violated his ethical obligation to give credit to
this student, who contributed to his article, in a manner that was in accordance with that stu-
dent’s contributions. Standard G.5.f. of the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) states that manuscripts
that are substantially based on a student’s course paper, project, dissertation, or thesis must be
used only with the student’s permission and must list the student as lead author. If the contri-
butions of Lakiesha’s paper to Dr. Wilks’s article were less substantial, they would still merit an
acknowledgment (§G.5.d.). Lakiesha is in an uncomfortable position due to the power dynamics
at work here. Rather than letting the matter drop, we would recommend that Lakiesha talk with a
trusted mentor who is not on her examination committee and explain the incident without iden-
tifying Dr. Wilks. If Lakiesha decides, after consultation, to take some kind of action, she should
talk first with Dr. Wilks despite the discomfort she might experience. It is possible that Dr. Wilks
wrote the paper he published based only on his own independent research, which happened to
be similar to the content of the paper Lakiesha wrote.

-- 407 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	385
then as graduates, and finally as colleagues and professional peers of their trainers (Hays et al.,
2021). These transitions shift the balance of power but do not eliminate it. Professors have a degree
of power over former students for a long time, as they are asked throughout the careers of former
students for recommendations for jobs, certifications, and licenses.
It has been argued that students are in an even more vulnerable position than are clients with
respect to dual relationships. Whereas clients can terminate their relationships with their counselors,
students do not have the same option.
Relationship boundaries in counselor education are somewhat fluid because good teach-
ing at the graduate level often involves mentoring and opportunities for faculty and students to
interact in professional and social settings such as colloquiums, receptions, and special events.
Mentoring is typically viewed in the literature as a positive kind of dual relationship that is
encouraged in academia because of its potential benefits to students. Nonetheless, even mentor-
ing involves potential risks, such as personal disillusionment with the relationship, professional
repercussions when the mentoring relationship falters, and the possible sexualization of the rela-
tionship. Male counselor educators experience unique relationship challenges within the context
of power relationships and potential claims of sexual harassment. The majority of counseling stu-
dents are female, and male counselor educators are particularly sensitive to even the appearance
of impropriety. Ray et al. (2016) identified more than 40 strategies that male counselor educa-
tors use to structure their relationships with students, including not meeting alone with students,
meeting only on campus, making no physical contact, and mentioning their significant others and
family in classes or conversations.
Despite complicating factors, counselor educators can take some steps to maintain healthy
boundaries with students. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) contains several standards that describe
these steps under §F.10.: Roles and Relationships Between Counselor Educators and Students.
These standards are summarized as follows:
• Counselor educators are prohibited from having sexual or romantic interactions or relationships
with students currently enrolled in a counseling program and over whom they have power and
authority. This prohibition applies to electronic as well as in-person interactions (§F.10.a.).
• Counselor educators do not condone or subject students to sexual harassment (§F.10.b.). Sexual
harassment is illegal as well as unethical; Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972
prohibits sexual harassment, which occurs when students are pressured by unwanted sexual
overtures or acts.
• Nonprofessional relationships with former students are not prohibited, but counselor educators
must be aware of the power differential that continues to exist. Whenever counselor educators
consider engaging in social, sexual, or other intimate relationships with a former student, they
must discuss the potential risks with the former student (§F.10.c.).
• Counselor educators should avoid having nonacademic relationships with students in which
there is a risk of potential harm to the student or that may compromise the training experience
or grades assigned (§F.10.d.).
• If a counselor educator believes a nonprofessional relationship may be beneficial to a student,
the educator should take precautions similar to those taken by counselors when working with
clients. In addition, counselor educators should “discuss with students the rationale for such
interactions, the potential benefits and drawbacks, and the anticipated consequences for the
student,” “clarify the specific nature and limitations of the additional role(s) they will have
with the student prior to engaging in a nonprofessional relationship,” and have only nonpro-
fessional relationships that are “time-limited and/or context specific and initiated with student
consent” (§F.10.f.).

-- 408 of 593 --

386 	Section 2 • Issues
SEXUAL OR ROMANTIC RELATIONSHIPS 	Although the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) prohibits
sexual relationships between counselor educators and current students, consensual sexual relation-
ships between adult students and professors generally are not prohibited by law. Arguments have
been presented by those who believe that these relationships should be prohibited, asserting that
the differential in power between students and professors makes it impossible for relationships to
be truly consensual. Those who argue that such relationships should be allowed have contended
that prohibiting such relationships would infringe on constitutional rights of free association, would
inappropriately invade the privacy of faculty and students, or would infantilize or disempower adult
students by denying them the right to make their own choices. Historically, concern about the harm
caused by sexual relationships has been a concern primarily of faculty in counseling and psychology
programs, but there is some indication that this concern is spreading: Research conducted by Abboud
et al. (2018) revealed that 29 states had enacted specific educator sexual misconduct statutes by
2017, but many of these statutes apply only when the students are minors. In 2023, 39 states and the
District of Columbia had adopted statutes that prohibit sexual abuse of schoolchildren, and 75% of
U.S. states and jurisdictions have passed statutes that specifically outlaw educator sexual misconduct
even when the student is over the age of consent (Enough Abuse Campaign, 2023). During the 1990s,
several universities implemented policies that forbid all professors and administrators from dating
the students they teach, mentor, or supervise (James, 1996; Leatherman, 1993), and that number has
likely increased in the twenty-first century. Richards and Nystrom (2022), in their examination of
policies of institutions of higher education, found that universities are becoming more restrictive in
their policies, moving toward limited bans and prohibitions and including graduate students. Most
policies place the burden of proof on faculty to prove consent when allegations are made.
Lawsuits brought by students against college and university professors because of consensual
personal relationships probably would not be successful because these relationships are not prohib-
ited by law. However, because teaching counseling skills requires personal reflection on the part of
students and requires counselor educators to assess students in personal as well as academic areas,
an argument could be made that professor–student personal relationships in counseling programs
have more potential for harm than such relationships in other academic disciplines (Kress & Dixon,
2007). In addition, the specific sections in the Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) that prohibit sexual
relationships with students (§F.10.a.) or sexual harassment of students (§F.10.b.) would seem to
give students a foundation for prevailing in a lawsuit when they believe they have been harmed by a
professor who had a personal relationship with them.
The question of whether a sexual relationship between a counselor educator and a student
can truly be consensual and noncoercive takes on some new dimensions when viewed from an ethi-
cal perspective. From this perspective, sexual relationships between faculty and students are never
appropriate due to the power differential inherent in the relationship.
NONSEXUAL RELATIONSHIPS 	Although counselor educators should not enter formal, therapeutic
relationships with students, the very nature of counselor training presents many opportunities for pro-
fessors to get to know students at a personal level. Students may self-disclose personal issues during
a live demonstration of counseling, a dyadic practice session in which one student is serving as the
counselor and the other as a client, or an experiential component of a group counseling course. This
could also happen when a counselor educator is exploring a practicum student’s difficulties in work-
ing with a particular client or in courses in which students are encouraged to explore any personal
biases, prejudices, or values that might interfere with their ability to counsel effectively. Students
who engage in self-exploration do so in the belief that their professors will not violate their trust.
Thus, counselor educators have a special responsibility to be trustworthy recipients of the knowledge

-- 409 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	387
they gain about students’ personal and interpersonal struggles. This trustworthiness is as important in
the student–educator relationship as it is in the client–counselor relationship.
Certain kinds of dual relationships are inevitable in counselor training programs. However,
there is no universal agreement regarding how these situations can best be handled. Counselor edu-
cators mentor graduate students, socialize with them at professional conferences, and interact with
them in myriad ways outside the formal classroom setting. Welfel (2019) and Herlihy and Corey
(2015b) have suggested that some flexibility is needed, arguing that when faculty take a rigid stance
against all forms of dual relationships with students, they are probably overreacting to the risks.
Because new issues related to dual relationships are continually emerging, it is important for coun-
selor educators to have a thoughtfully considered model for decision making about such issues.
In considering the boundaries of relationships with students, counselor educators need to acknowl-
edge their position of power and authority over students, and a climate for ethical behavior should be
fostered. Counselor educators function as role models for the profession. Students learn about dual
relationships not only from explicit ethics education but also from their own experiences. Counselor
educators thus have a crucial responsibility to model ethical management of relationships with students
if they hope to foster ethical attitudes and behaviors in students (Herlihy & Corey, 2015b).
Negotiation of boundaries should be a shared endeavor, and it will be helpful for you as a
student to consider your own stance toward dual relationships with faculty members. You might ask
yourself the following questions when you consider entering into a dual relationship with a coun-
selor educator:
• Has my professor fully discussed with me the potential risks and benefits of our dual relation-
ship? Do we have a clear working agreement?
• What am I learning in this relationship? Am I becoming more competent or more dependent
on a special relationship?
• What are my fellow students learning? Are they learning about equitable treatment or about
special privilege?
• Do I have a choice? Do I feel that I have the freedom to refuse requests the professor might
make or to withdraw from the relationship without penalty?
• Do I believe the professor has maintained the capacity to evaluate me objectively?
Diversity Considerations in Faculty–Student Relationships
Mentoring is the one form of role blending that is almost universally viewed as positive and important
to the professional development of counselor trainees. Mentors serve as coaches, guides, role models,
sources of support (Purgason et al., 2018), advisors, and sponsors into the profession upon graduation.
Doctoral students who have opportunities to conduct collaborative research, teaching, and profes-
sional services are enriched by the experience and are better prepared to enter the professorate.
Mentoring relationships are a highly valued component of counselor training, especially at
the doctoral level (Perera-Diltz & Sauerheber, 2017), and are a key ingredient in developing mul-
ticultural leadership (Storlie et al., 2015). Because there are so few counselor educators who are
non-White, however, it may be difficult for graduate students who are persons of color to find a
mentor with whom they identify and feel comfortable in the close and complex mentoring relation-
ship. Brooks et al. (2023) urged counselor educators to create a safe and supportive environment
for Black students and to provide structured and culturally sensitive mentoring, and Cartwright
et al. (2021) provided recommendations for creating mentorship programs for Black women in
counselor education programs. It is essential that counselor educators are effective cross-cultural
mentors. Oller and Teeting (2021) have offered concrete recommendations for counselor education

-- 410 of 593 --

388 	Section 2 • Issues
programs, including cross-cultural awareness training for both faculty members and new graduate
students, education about the benefits of mentorship, acknowledgment of the obstacles faced by
marginalized students and faculty in counselor education programs, and developing and supporting
explicit cross-cultural mentoring guidelines for faculty–student dyads. Counselor education faculty
members can also encourage experienced doctoral students to mentor doctoral students new to the
program as well as master’s-level students.
Relationships Among Students
In counseling techniques courses, students are sometimes asked to be clients for their fellow stu-
dents as they practice their counseling skills. To prevent unrealistic role playing, students some-
times believe they should discuss issues to which they can relate from personal experience. To
prevent inappropriate self-disclosures, counselor educators who ask students to be clients for
other students in counseling skills courses should caution all course members to maintain confi-
dentiality regarding issues discussed in class and should intervene if student clients appear uneasy
or distressed.
When counselor education programs offer the doctorate degree as well as the master’s degree,
doctoral students frequently are involved in supervising the skills development of beginning students.
The doctoral students, in turn, receive supervision of their supervision by faculty. This practice, known
as layered supervision, offers many advantages: When motivated and enthusiastic doctoral students
assist with supervision, master’s students receive more supervision and high-quality supervision.
Doctoral students may have more focused energy and effort to devote to supervision, compared with
faculty members, who have many other responsibilities. Students learn to give and receive corrective
feedback and to manage multiple roles. At the same time, this practice places some complex demands
on doctoral students, who must move in and out of the multiple roles of student, supervisee, supervi-
sor, mentor, protégé, and peer/classmate (Dickens et al., 2016). They are in a position of authority
over their master’s student supervisees but may not feel empowered in that role because they are still
students under the supervision of faculty. The Code of Ethics (ACA, 2014) addresses these concerns
by saying that counselor educators should make efforts to ensure that the rights of students are not
compromised when their peers lead experiential counseling activities (§F.7.g.). Counselor educators
need to impress upon students who lead groups composed of other students, or who supervise other
students, that they have the same ethical obligations that counselor educators, trainers, and supervisors
have. Faculty must carefully supervise student group leaders and student supervisors to help them
understand that multiple relationships in counselor education programs are inevitable and manageable
and to help them navigate these relationships and resolve problems that occur.
14-4 The Case of Luis
Luis, age 26, received his master’s degree a year ago and immediately applied for admission and
was accepted into the doctoral program in counselor education at the same university. He has
been assigned to supervise Ella, a 45-year-old master’s-degree student, as Ella begins her coun-
seling internship. Luis and Ella were in two classes together when Luis was still in the master’s
program, although they did not know each other very well. Despite Luis’s best efforts to establish
a supervisory alliance, Ella becomes increasingly resistant to supervision. Luis shares their dif-
ficulties with his faculty supervisor and wonders whether Ella should be assigned to a different
doctoral student supervisor.

-- 411 of 593 --

Chapter 14 • Issues in Counselor Education 	389
• What are some reasons Ella may be resistant to being supervised by Luis?
• What do you think the faculty supervisor should do in this circumstance?
Discussion: Ella may be finding it difficult to accept the fact that Luis, who is someone she has
known as a peer and who is much younger, is now in a supervisory capacity over her. Luis’s fac-
ulty supervisor might suggest that Luis address this dynamic directly with Ella. If her resistance
to supervision continues, the faculty member should intervene and try to do so in a way that will
make this into a positive learning experience for both Ella and Luis.
Responsibilities of Students
Reading this chapter has made you aware that students have many rights. For example, you are
entitled, under the law, to due process and freedom from discrimination. Section F of the Code
of Ethics (ACA, 2014) contains numerous standards concerning the duties of counselor educa-
tors to ensure that your welfare is safeguarded. However, along with your rights come certain
